flashcards.answer: The temperature at which air becomes saturated and water vapor begins to condense

flashcards.question: Condensation Nuclei
flashcards.answer: Tiny particles (dust, salt, pollen) on which water vapor condenses to form cloud droplets

flashcards.question: Cirrus
flashcards.answer: High, thin, wispy ice-crystal clouds indicating fair weather or approaching warm front

flashcards.question: Cumulonimbus
flashcards.answer: Massive vertical cloud producing thunderstorms, heavy rain, lightning, and possibly hail or tornadoes

flashcards.question: Bergeron Process
flashcards.answer: Precipitation formation in cold clouds where ice crystals grow at the expense of supercooled water droplets

quiz_questions.question_text: Clouds form when rising air cools to its:
quiz_questions.explanation: When air cools to the dew point, it becomes saturated and water vapor condenses into tiny droplets, forming clouds.

quiz_questions.question_text: Cumulonimbus clouds are associated with:

options.text: Thunderstorms, heavy rain, and possibly hail or tornadoes

quiz_questions.explanation: Cumulonimbus clouds extend vertically through multiple atmospheric levels and produce severe weather including thunderstorms.

quiz_questions.question_text: Cirrus clouds are found at _____ altitudes and are made of:
quiz_questions.explanation: Cirrus clouds form above 6 km where temperatures are well below freezing, so they consist of ice crystals.

quiz_questions.question_text: Condensation nuclei are important because:

options.text: They provide surfaces on which water vapor condenses to form cloud droplets

quiz_questions.explanation: Without condensation nuclei (dust, salt, pollen), water vapor wouldn't readily condense into the droplets that make up clouds.

quiz_questions.question_text: Nimbostratus clouds produce:
quiz_questions.explanation: Nimbostratus are thick, dark, low clouds that bring prolonged steady rain or snow over wide areas.

quiz_questions.question_text: Sleet forms when:

options.text: Raindrops freeze into ice pellets before reaching the ground
Snow melts and refreezes at high altitude

quiz_questions.explanation: Sleet occurs when rain falls through a freezing layer above the ground and turns into ice pellets before landing.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Bergeron process produces precipitation in cold clouds by:

options.text: Ice crystals growing at the expense of supercooled water droplets

quiz_questions.explanation: In mixed-phase clouds, ice crystals attract water vapor from supercooled droplets (which shrink), growing heavy enough to fall.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Weather Fronts & Storms
summary_sections.content_html:
A front is the boundary between two air masses of different temperature and humidity.

  • Cold front: cold air pushes under warm air, forcing it up rapidly → towering clouds, heavy rain/thunderstorms, then clearing and cooler temperatures
  • Warm front: warm air slides over retreating cold air → gradual cloud buildup, steady light precipitation
  • Stationary front: neither air mass advances → prolonged cloudiness and precipitation
  • Occluded front: a cold front overtakes a warm front → complex weather, precipitation

Storms:

  • Thunderstorms: caused by strong convection; produce lightning, heavy rain, wind, sometimes hail and tornadoes
  • Hurricanes (tropical cyclones): form over warm ocean water (≥26.5°C); categories 1–5 on Saffir-Simpson scale
  • Tornadoes: violently rotating columns of air; measured on Enhanced Fujita (EF) scale

flashcards.question: Cold Front
flashcards.answer: Boundary where advancing cold air pushes beneath warmer air, causing rapid uplift and storms

flashcards.question: Warm Front
flashcards.answer: Boundary where advancing warm air slides over retreating cold air, causing gradual precipitation

flashcards.question: Hurricane
flashcards.answer: A large tropical cyclone with sustained winds ≥74 mph, fueled by warm ocean water

flashcards.question: Tornado
flashcards.answer: A violently rotating column of air extending from a thunderstorm to the ground

flashcards.question: Air Mass
flashcards.answer: A large body of air with relatively uniform temperature and humidity characteristics

quiz_questions.question_text: At a cold front, weather typically includes:

options.text: Rapid cloud buildup, heavy storms, then clearing and cooler temperatures

quiz_questions.explanation: Cold air forcefully undercuts warm air, causing rapid uplift that produces towering clouds and intense but short-lived storms.

quiz_questions.question_text: Hurricanes form over:
quiz_questions.explanation: Hurricanes require warm ocean water to evaporate and fuel the storm's energy through latent heat release.

quiz_questions.question_text: A stationary front produces:

options.text: Prolonged cloudiness and precipitation in one area

quiz_questions.explanation: Since neither air mass is advancing, the front stalls, keeping clouds and precipitation over the same area for days.

quiz_questions.question_text: Tornadoes are measured on the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The EF scale rates tornadoes 0–5 based on estimated wind speeds and observed damage.

quiz_questions.question_text: At a warm front, precipitation is generally:

options.text: Light and steady over a wide area

quiz_questions.explanation: Warm air gently slides over cold air, producing a gradual buildup of clouds and widespread, light steady precipitation.

quiz_questions.question_text: Lightning forms in thunderstorms due to:

options.text: Build-up and discharge of electrical charge within cumulonimbus clouds

quiz_questions.explanation: Charge separation occurs within towering cumulonimbus clouds; lightning is the discharge that equalizes the charge difference.

quiz_questions.question_text: An occluded front forms when:

options.text: A cold front overtakes a warm front

quiz_questions.explanation: In an occluded front, a faster-moving cold front catches up to a warm front, lifting the warm air mass off the ground entirely.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Climate vs. Weather
summary_sections.content_html:
Weather is the short-term state of the atmosphere at a specific place and time (temperature, precipitation, wind, humidity).

Climate is the average of weather conditions over a long period (usually 30+ years) for a region.

  • Weather changes day to day; climate represents long-term patterns and expectations.
  • Climate is described by average temperature, precipitation, seasonal patterns, and extreme events.
  • 'Climate is what you expect; weather is what you get.'

flashcards.question: Weather
flashcards.answer: Short-term atmospheric conditions at a specific place and time

flashcards.question: Climate
flashcards.answer: The average pattern of weather conditions over 30+ years for a region

flashcards.question: Meteorology
flashcards.answer: The scientific study of weather and short-term atmospheric phenomena

flashcards.question: Climatology
flashcards.answer: The scientific study of long-term weather patterns and climate

flashcards.question: Normal
flashcards.answer: The 30-year average of a weather variable (temperature, precipitation) for a location

quiz_questions.question_text: Climate differs from weather in that climate is:

options.text: The long-term average of weather patterns over 30+ years

quiz_questions.explanation: Climate represents average weather conditions measured over decades, while weather is the current or short-term state.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 30-year average temperature for a city represents its:
quiz_questions.explanation: Climate normals are the 30-year averages of weather variables used to characterize a region's climate.

quiz_questions.question_text: 'It's unusually cold today' describes:
quiz_questions.explanation: A single day's temperature is weather—a short-term condition—not a long-term climate pattern.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement describes climate?

options.text: Summers in Arizona are typically hot and dry

quiz_questions.explanation: 'Typically hot and dry summers' describes a long-term pattern expected for a region—that's climate.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why do scientists use 30-year averages to define climate?

options.text: 30 years smooths out year-to-year variability while capturing meaningful trends

quiz_questions.explanation: A 30-year window is long enough to average out short-term fluctuations but short enough to detect real shifts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Climatology is the study of:
quiz_questions.explanation: Climatology focuses on understanding long-term atmospheric patterns, climate zones, and climate change.

quiz_questions.question_text: A region's climate is described by:

options.text: Average temperature, precipitation, seasonal patterns, and extreme events over decades

quiz_questions.explanation: Climate is a comprehensive description including averages and variability of multiple weather variables over long periods.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Factors Affecting Climate
summary_sections.content_html:
Several factors determine the climate of a region:

  • Latitude: the most important factor; higher latitudes receive less direct solar energy → cooler climates
  • Altitude: temperature decreases ~6.5°C per 1,000 m elevation gain
  • Ocean currents: warm currents (Gulf Stream) moderate coastal climates; cold currents cool and dry adjacent coasts
  • Distance from the ocean (continentality): oceans moderate temperature; interiors have more extreme seasonal swings
  • Topography: mountain ranges create rain shadow effects—moist air rises on the windward side (rain), descends as dry air on the leeward side (desert)
  • Prevailing winds: determine moisture transport and precipitation patterns

flashcards.question: Latitude
flashcards.answer: Angular distance from the equator; the primary factor determining a region's climate

flashcards.question: Rain Shadow
flashcards.answer: A dry area on the leeward side of a mountain range where descending air loses moisture

flashcards.question: Continentality
flashcards.answer: The effect of distance from oceans on climate; interior regions have more extreme temperatures

flashcards.question: Gulf Stream
flashcards.answer: A warm ocean current that moderates the climate of western Europe

flashcards.question: Lapse Rate
flashcards.answer: The rate of temperature decrease with altitude (~6.5°C per 1,000 m in the troposphere)

quiz_questions.question_text: The most important factor affecting a region's climate is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Latitude determines the angle and intensity of received solar radiation—the fundamental driver of climate differences.

quiz_questions.question_text: A rain shadow desert forms on the _____ side of a mountain range.
quiz_questions.explanation: Moist air rises on the windward side and drops precipitation; the dry air descends on the leeward side, creating arid conditions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Coastal cities have milder temperature swings than inland cities because:

options.text: Water has a high heat capacity and moderates temperatures

quiz_questions.explanation: Water heats and cools more slowly than land, so oceans buffer coastal temperatures, reducing seasonal extremes.

quiz_questions.question_text: Temperature decreases with altitude at approximately:
quiz_questions.explanation: The environmental lapse rate averages about 6.5°C per 1,000 m of elevation gain in the troposphere.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Gulf Stream affects European climate by:

options.text: Carrying warm water across the Atlantic, warming western Europe

quiz_questions.explanation: The Gulf Stream transports warm tropical water northeastward, making western Europe's climate much milder than its latitude would suggest.

quiz_questions.question_text: Prevailing winds affect climate by:

options.text: Transporting moisture and heat, influencing precipitation patterns

quiz_questions.explanation: Prevailing winds carry moisture from oceans to land and distribute heat, significantly influencing regional precipitation and temperature.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which city would likely have more extreme temperature changes throughout the year?

options.text: A landlocked city far from oceans

quiz_questions.explanation: Inland cities lack the moderating influence of nearby water and experience greater seasonal temperature extremes (high continentality).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Climate Zones & Biomes
summary_sections.content_html:
Earth's climates are classified into zones that correspond to distinct biomes (large ecological regions):

  • Tropical (0°–23.5°): warm year-round, high rainfall → tropical rainforests, savannas
  • Dry/Arid: low precipitation → deserts, steppes
  • Temperate (~23.5°–66.5°): moderate temperatures, distinct seasons → deciduous forests, Mediterranean
  • Continental (interior, mid-latitudes): large seasonal temperature swings → prairies, boreal forests
  • Polar (>66.5°): very cold, low precipitation → tundra, ice caps

The Köppen climate classification system uses temperature and precipitation data to define climate types (A=tropical, B=arid, C=temperate, D=continental, E=polar).

flashcards.question: Biome
flashcards.answer: A large ecological region characterized by its climate, plants, and animals

flashcards.question: Tropical Rainforest
flashcards.answer: A biome near the equator with year-round warmth, heavy rainfall, and the highest biodiversity

flashcards.question: Tundra
flashcards.answer: A cold, treeless biome near the poles with permafrost and limited vegetation

flashcards.question: Köppen Classification
flashcards.answer: A widely used system that categorizes climates based on temperature and precipitation patterns

flashcards.question: Savanna
flashcards.answer: A tropical grassland biome with scattered trees, wet and dry seasons

quiz_questions.question_text: The Köppen climate classification categorizes climates based on:
quiz_questions.explanation: The Köppen system uses average monthly temperature and precipitation data to classify climates into five main groups.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which biome has the highest biodiversity?
quiz_questions.explanation: Tropical rainforests contain more than half of the world's species due to year-round warmth, moisture, and stable conditions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Tundra biomes are characterized by:

options.text: Very cold temperatures, permafrost, and treeless vegetation

quiz_questions.explanation: The tundra is too cold for trees; the ground contains permafrost, and vegetation consists of mosses, lichens, and low shrubs.

quiz_questions.question_text: Continental climate zones experience:

options.text: Large differences between summer and winter temperatures

quiz_questions.explanation: Continental climates (found in mid-latitude interiors) have hot summers and cold winters—large seasonal swings.

quiz_questions.question_text: Savannas are found in:

options.text: Tropical regions with distinct wet and dry seasons

quiz_questions.explanation: Savannas are tropical grasslands with scattered trees, experiencing pronounced wet and dry seasons.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the Köppen system, 'B' climates represent:
quiz_questions.explanation: The letter B designates arid climates where evaporation exceeds precipitation—deserts and semi-arid steppes.

quiz_questions.question_text: The primary factor determining which biome exists in a region is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Climate is the master factor—temperature and precipitation patterns determine which plant communities and therefore which biome develops.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: The Greenhouse Effect
summary_sections.content_html:
The greenhouse effect is a natural process that warms Earth's surface:

  • Sunlight (short-wave radiation) passes through the atmosphere and warms Earth's surface.
  • Earth re-emits energy as infrared (long-wave) radiation.
  • Greenhouse gases (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, H₂O vapor, O₃) absorb and re-emit infrared radiation, trapping heat in the atmosphere.
  • Without the greenhouse effect, Earth's average temperature would be about -18°C instead of +15°C.
  • The enhanced greenhouse effect: human activities (burning fossil fuels, deforestation, agriculture) increase greenhouse gas concentrations, trapping more heat and causing global warming.

flashcards.question: Greenhouse Effect
flashcards.answer: The natural warming of Earth when greenhouse gases trap outgoing infrared radiation

flashcards.question: Greenhouse Gas
flashcards.answer: An atmospheric gas that absorbs and re-emits infrared radiation (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, H₂O vapor)

flashcards.question: Infrared Radiation
flashcards.answer: Long-wave heat energy emitted by Earth's warm surface

flashcards.question: Enhanced Greenhouse Effect
flashcards.answer: The intensification of natural warming due to human-caused increases in greenhouse gases

flashcards.question: Global Warming
flashcards.answer: The observed increase in Earth's average temperature, primarily from the enhanced greenhouse effect

quiz_questions.question_text: The greenhouse effect is:

options.text: A natural process that warms Earth to habitable temperatures

quiz_questions.explanation: The greenhouse effect is natural and essential—without it, Earth would be about -18°C. Human activities are enhancing it beyond natural levels.

quiz_questions.question_text: Without the greenhouse effect, Earth's average temperature would be approximately:
quiz_questions.explanation: Greenhouse gases raise Earth's temperature from about -18°C (what it would be without them) to its current average of about 15°C.

quiz_questions.question_text: Greenhouse gases trap heat by:

options.text: Absorbing and re-emitting infrared (heat) radiation emitted by Earth's surface

quiz_questions.explanation: Greenhouse gases are transparent to incoming sunlight but absorb outgoing infrared radiation, re-emitting it back toward the surface.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is NOT a greenhouse gas?
quiz_questions.explanation: N₂ makes up 78% of the atmosphere but is not a greenhouse gas—it doesn't absorb infrared radiation effectively.

quiz_questions.question_text: The enhanced greenhouse effect is caused by:

options.text: Human activities increasing greenhouse gas concentrations

quiz_questions.explanation: Burning fossil fuels, deforestation, and agriculture have increased CO₂ and CH₄ concentrations, intensifying the natural greenhouse effect.

quiz_questions.question_text: Earth's surface emits energy primarily as:
quiz_questions.explanation: Earth absorbs short-wave sunlight and re-emits it as longer-wavelength infrared radiation (heat), which greenhouse gases can trap.

quiz_questions.question_text: Water vapor is a greenhouse gas that is unique because:

options.text: Its concentration depends on temperature (positive feedback: warming → more evaporation → more trapping)

quiz_questions.explanation: Water vapor amplifies warming: as temperatures rise, more water evaporates, increasing water vapor concentrations and trapping more heat.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Evidence of Climate Change
summary_sections.content_html:
Multiple independent lines of evidence confirm that global climate is changing:

  • Temperature records: global average has risen ~1.1°C since the late 1800s; 20 of the 21 warmest years on record have occurred since 2000
  • Ice cores: air bubbles trapped in ancient ice reveal past CO₂ levels and temperatures over 800,000 years
  • Sea-level rise: ~20 cm since 1900 due to thermal expansion and melting ice
  • Glacial retreat: glaciers worldwide are shrinking
  • Arctic sea ice decline: summer minimum extent has decreased ~40% since 1979
  • Ocean acidification: CO₂ dissolving in seawater lowers pH
  • Ecosystem changes: shifting species ranges, earlier spring events, coral bleaching

flashcards.question: Ice Core
flashcards.answer: A cylinder of ice drilled from glaciers or ice sheets that contains trapped air bubbles recording past atmospheric conditions

flashcards.question: Thermal Expansion
flashcards.answer: The increase in water volume as temperature rises, contributing to sea-level rise

flashcards.question: Ocean Acidification
flashcards.answer: The decrease in ocean pH caused by absorption of atmospheric CO₂

flashcards.question: Glacial Retreat
flashcards.answer: The shrinking of glaciers due to more ice melting than accumulating each year

flashcards.question: Proxy Data
flashcards.answer: Indirect records of past climate (ice cores, tree rings, ocean sediments, pollen) used to reconstruct climate history

quiz_questions.question_text: Global average temperature has increased approximately _____ since the late 1800s.
quiz_questions.explanation: Multiple independent temperature records show an increase of about 1.1°C (2°F) since the pre-industrial era.

quiz_questions.question_text: Ice cores provide evidence of past climate by:

options.text: Trapping air bubbles that preserve ancient atmospheric composition and temperature indicators

quiz_questions.explanation: Air bubbles in ice cores preserve samples of ancient atmospheres, allowing scientists to measure past CO₂ levels and temperature proxies.

quiz_questions.question_text: Sea levels have risen approximately _____ since 1900.
quiz_questions.explanation: Global mean sea level has risen about 20 cm since 1900, accelerating in recent decades.

quiz_questions.question_text: Ocean acidification is caused by:

options.text: Industrial chemicals directly dumped in oceans
CO₂ from the atmosphere dissolving in seawater, forming carbonic acid

quiz_questions.explanation: Seawater absorbs atmospheric CO₂, which reacts with water to form carbonic acid, lowering ocean pH.

quiz_questions.question_text: Arctic sea ice has declined since 1979, with summer minimums decreasing by roughly:
quiz_questions.explanation: Satellite data shows Arctic summer sea ice extent has declined by about 40% since systematic measurements began in 1979.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is an observed biological response to climate change?

options.text: Species shifting their ranges toward the poles and to higher elevations

quiz_questions.explanation: As temperatures rise, many species are moving poleward or uphill to track suitable climate conditions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Proxy data for reconstructing past climates includes all EXCEPT:
quiz_questions.explanation: Proxy data are indirect natural records. Internet weather databases are modern instruments, not proxy data—the internet didn't exist before the late 20th century.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Human Impact on Climate
summary_sections.content_html:
Human activities are the primary driver of current climate change:

  • Burning fossil fuels: coal, oil, natural gas combustion releases CO₂—the largest source of greenhouse gas emissions (~75%)
  • Deforestation: removes carbon sinks (trees absorb CO₂); burning/decomposing trees release stored carbon
  • Agriculture: livestock produce CH₄; rice paddies release CH₄; fertilizers release N₂O
  • Industry: cement production, manufacturing processes release CO₂ and other gases

Mitigation strategies: renewable energy, energy efficiency, reforestation, carbon capture, international agreements (Paris Agreement), reducing consumption

Adaptation strategies: building sea walls, developing drought-resistant crops, improving warning systems, urban planning

flashcards.question: Fossil Fuel
flashcards.answer: Coal, oil, or natural gas formed from ancient organic matter; burning releases CO₂

flashcards.question: Carbon Sink
flashcards.answer: A natural system (forests, oceans) that absorbs more CO₂ than it releases

flashcards.question: Deforestation
flashcards.answer: Clearing of forests, removing carbon sinks and releasing stored carbon

flashcards.question: Paris Agreement
flashcards.answer: An international treaty (2015) committing nations to limit global warming to well below 2°C

flashcards.question: Carbon Capture
flashcards.answer: Technologies that capture CO₂ emissions from power plants or the atmosphere for storage

quiz_questions.question_text: The largest source of human-caused greenhouse gas emissions is:

options.text: Burning fossil fuels (coal, oil, natural gas)

quiz_questions.explanation: Fossil fuel combustion for energy and transportation accounts for approximately 75% of human-caused greenhouse gas emissions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Deforestation contributes to climate change because:

options.text: Removing trees eliminates carbon sinks and releases stored carbon

quiz_questions.explanation: Forests absorb CO₂; cutting them removes this carbon sink and releases the carbon stored in trees back into the atmosphere.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Paris Agreement aims to:

options.text: Limit global warming to well below 2°C above pre-industrial levels

quiz_questions.explanation: The 2015 Paris Agreement set targets to hold warming below 2°C (ideally 1.5°C) through nationally determined contributions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which agricultural activity produces methane?

options.text: Livestock digestion and rice paddy cultivation

quiz_questions.explanation: Cattle produce CH₄ through enteric fermentation, and flooded rice paddies create anaerobic conditions where CH₄-producing bacteria thrive.

quiz_questions.question_text: A carbon sink is:

options.text: A natural system that absorbs more CO₂ than it releases

quiz_questions.explanation: Forests and oceans are major carbon sinks, absorbing billions of tons of CO₂ annually from the atmosphere.

quiz_questions.question_text: An example of climate adaptation (not mitigation) is:

options.text: Building sea walls to protect coastal communities from rising sea levels

quiz_questions.explanation: Adaptation means adjusting to the effects of climate change (sea walls, drought-resistant crops), while mitigation means reducing the causes.

quiz_questions.question_text: Renewable energy helps address climate change by:

options.text: Generating electricity without burning fossil fuels, reducing greenhouse gas emissions

quiz_questions.explanation: Solar, wind, and other renewables produce energy without the CO₂ emissions from fossil fuel combustion.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Ocean Floor Features
summary_sections.content_html:
The ocean floor has distinct topographic features shaped by plate tectonics:

  • Continental shelf: shallow, gently sloping extension of the continent underwater (avg. depth ~130 m)
  • Continental slope: steep drop-off from the shelf to the deep ocean
  • Abyssal plain: vast, flat, deep ocean floor (3,000–6,000 m depth) covered in fine sediment
  • Mid-ocean ridge: underwater mountain chain formed at divergent plate boundaries (longest mountain range on Earth)
  • Ocean trench: deep, narrow depression at convergent boundaries (e.g., Mariana Trench ~11,000 m deep)
  • Seamounts: underwater volcanic mountains; flat-topped ones called guyots

flashcards.question: Continental Shelf
flashcards.answer: The shallow, gently sloping underwater extension of a continent (avg. depth ~130 m)

flashcards.question: Abyssal Plain
flashcards.answer: A vast, flat region of the deep ocean floor (3,000–6,000 m) covered in fine sediment

flashcards.question: Mid-Ocean Ridge
flashcards.answer: The longest mountain chain on Earth, formed at divergent plate boundaries on the ocean floor

flashcards.question: Ocean Trench
flashcards.answer: A deep, narrow canyon in the ocean floor formed at convergent plate boundaries

flashcards.question: Seamount
flashcards.answer: An underwater volcanic mountain rising from the ocean floor; if flat-topped, called a guyot

quiz_questions.question_text: The continental shelf is:

options.text: The deepest part of the ocean
The shallow, gently sloping underwater extension of a continent

quiz_questions.explanation: Continental shelves extend from shorelines to a depth of about 130 m where the continental slope begins.

quiz_questions.question_text: The deepest point in the ocean is found in:
quiz_questions.explanation: The Mariana Trench reaches ~11,000 m deep—the deepest known point in the ocean—formed at a convergent boundary.

quiz_questions.question_text: Mid-ocean ridges form at:
quiz_questions.explanation: Mid-ocean ridges form where tectonic plates pull apart, allowing magma to rise and create new oceanic crust.

quiz_questions.question_text: Abyssal plains are characterized by:

options.text: Vast, flat, deep-sea areas covered in fine sediment

quiz_questions.explanation: Abyssal plains are flat because fine sediment settles and smooths over any underlying topography at depths of 3,000–6,000 m.

quiz_questions.question_text: A guyot is a:

options.text: Flat-topped seamount (underwater volcano eroded to a flat summit)

quiz_questions.explanation: Guyots are seamounts whose peaks were eroded flat at the ocean surface before the seafloor subsided, carrying them below the waves.

quiz_questions.question_text: The longest mountain range on Earth is the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The global mid-ocean ridge system stretches over 65,000 km, making it far longer than any continental mountain range.

quiz_questions.question_text: Ocean trenches are associated with:
quiz_questions.explanation: Trenches form where one tectonic plate is subducted beneath another at convergent boundaries.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Ocean Water Properties
summary_sections.content_html:
Seawater's physical and chemical properties affect all marine processes:

  • Salinity: average ~35 parts per thousand (ppt); mainly NaCl. Varies with evaporation, precipitation, river input, and ice formation.
  • Temperature: ranges from -2°C (polar) to 30°C (tropical surface). Decreases with depth; the thermocline is the zone of rapid temperature change.
  • Density: depends on temperature (cold = denser) and salinity (saltier = denser). Drives deep ocean circulation.
  • Dissolved gases: O₂ (essential for marine life) and CO₂ (affects pH). Colder water holds more dissolved gas.
  • pH: normally ~8.1 (slightly alkaline); decreasing due to ocean acidification from absorbed CO₂.

flashcards.question: Salinity
flashcards.answer: The concentration of dissolved salts in seawater; average ~35 parts per thousand

flashcards.question: Thermocline
flashcards.answer: A layer of ocean water where temperature decreases rapidly with depth

flashcards.question: Density
flashcards.answer: Mass per unit volume; in seawater, increases with colder temperatures and higher salinity

flashcards.question: Dissolved Oxygen
flashcards.answer: Oxygen gas dissolved in water; essential for marine organisms to respire

flashcards.question: Ocean Acidification
flashcards.answer: The decrease in ocean pH from absorbing excess atmospheric CO₂

quiz_questions.question_text: The average salinity of ocean water is approximately:
quiz_questions.explanation: Average ocean salinity is about 35 parts per thousand (35 g of salt per liter), predominantly sodium chloride.

quiz_questions.question_text: The thermocline is the zone where:
quiz_questions.explanation: The thermocline is a transition layer (typically 200–1,000 m depth) where temperature drops sharply between warm surface water and cold deep water.

quiz_questions.question_text: Cold, salty water is _____ than warm, fresh water.
quiz_questions.explanation: Both lower temperature and higher salinity increase water density. Cold, salty water is the densest and sinks to the bottom.

quiz_questions.question_text: Colder water can hold _____ dissolved oxygen than warmer water.
quiz_questions.explanation: Gas solubility in water increases as temperature decreases, so cold polar waters hold more dissolved oxygen.

quiz_questions.question_text: Ocean pH is decreasing due to:

options.text: Absorption of excess atmospheric CO₂, forming carbonic acid

quiz_questions.explanation: Seawater absorbs CO₂, which reacts with water to form carbonic acid (H₂CO₃), lowering the ocean's pH.

quiz_questions.question_text: Salinity increases when:

options.text: Evaporation removes water but leaves salt behind

quiz_questions.explanation: Evaporation removes pure water from the ocean, increasing the concentration of dissolved salts in the remaining water.

quiz_questions.question_text: Deep ocean currents are primarily driven by:

options.text: Differences in water density (related to temperature and salinity)

quiz_questions.explanation: Thermohaline circulation is driven by density differences—cold, salty water sinks and flows along the ocean floor.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Ocean Currents & Circulation
summary_sections.content_html:
Ocean currents are large-scale movements of seawater driven by wind, density differences, and the Coriolis effect:

  • Surface currents (upper ~400 m): driven by prevailing winds. Form circular patterns called gyres—clockwise in Northern Hemisphere, counterclockwise in Southern.
  • Deep currents (thermohaline circulation): driven by density differences from temperature and salinity. Cold, salty water sinks at polar regions and flows along the ocean floor toward the equator.
  • Upwelling: cold, nutrient-rich deep water rises to the surface (often near coasts). Supports highly productive fisheries.
  • The global conveyor belt: a connected system of surface and deep currents that circulates water, heat, and nutrients around the globe.

flashcards.question: Gyre
flashcards.answer: A large circular pattern of ocean surface currents; clockwise in NH, counterclockwise in SH

flashcards.question: Thermohaline Circulation
flashcards.answer: Deep ocean circulation driven by differences in water density from temperature and salinity

flashcards.question: Upwelling
flashcards.answer: The rising of cold, nutrient-rich deep water to the ocean's surface

flashcards.question: Global Conveyor Belt
flashcards.answer: The interconnected system of surface and deep ocean currents circulating water worldwide

flashcards.question: Surface Current
flashcards.answer: An ocean current in the upper ~400 m driven primarily by wind

quiz_questions.question_text: Surface ocean currents are primarily driven by:
quiz_questions.explanation: Wind blowing across the ocean surface transfers energy, pushing water and creating surface currents.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the Northern Hemisphere, ocean gyres circulate:
quiz_questions.explanation: The Coriolis effect deflects currents to the right in the NH, creating clockwise-rotating gyres.

quiz_questions.question_text: Upwelling is important because it:

options.text: Brings cold, nutrient-rich water to the surface, supporting productive ecosystems

quiz_questions.explanation: Upwelling delivers nutrients from the deep to the surface, fueling phytoplankton growth and supporting rich fisheries.

quiz_questions.question_text: Thermohaline circulation is driven by:

options.text: Differences in water density caused by temperature and salinity

quiz_questions.explanation: 'Thermo' (temperature) and 'haline' (salt): cold, salty water is dense and sinks, driving the deep ocean conveyor belt.

quiz_questions.question_text: The global conveyor belt is critical for Earth's climate because it:

options.text: Distributes heat, nutrients, and dissolved gases around the globe

quiz_questions.explanation: The conveyor belt transports warm water from the tropics toward the poles and cold water back, redistributing heat globally.

quiz_questions.question_text: Where does deep water formation occur in the thermohaline circulation?

options.text: In polar regions where cold, salty water sinks

quiz_questions.explanation: In the North Atlantic and around Antarctica, surface water becomes cold and salty (from ice formation), becomes dense, and sinks.

quiz_questions.question_text: If thermohaline circulation weakened significantly, the likely result in western Europe would be:

options.text: Significant cooling because the Gulf Stream delivers less warm water

quiz_questions.explanation: Western Europe's mild climate depends on warm water delivered by the North Atlantic branch of the conveyor belt; weakening it would cause cooling.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Tides & Their Causes
summary_sections.content_html:
Tides are the regular rise and fall of ocean water levels caused by the gravitational pull of the Moon and Sun:

  • The Moon has the greatest tidal influence (closer to Earth than the Sun).
  • Tides produce two bulges: one facing the Moon (gravitational pull) and one on the opposite side (centrifugal effect).
  • Spring tides: Sun and Moon aligned (new moon, full moon) → largest tidal range (highest high tides, lowest low tides)
  • Neap tides: Sun and Moon at right angles (first/third quarter) → smallest tidal range
  • Most coasts experience two high tides and two low tides per day (semidiurnal), though some have diurnal (one cycle) or mixed patterns.
  • Tidal range varies with coastal geography—the Bay of Fundy has the world's largest tides (~16 m).

flashcards.question: Tide
flashcards.answer: The regular rise and fall of ocean water levels caused by gravitational forces of the Moon and Sun

flashcards.question: Spring Tide
flashcards.answer: Tides with the largest range occurring when the Sun and Moon are aligned (new/full moon)

flashcards.question: Neap Tide
flashcards.answer: Tides with the smallest range occurring when the Sun and Moon are at right angles (quarter moons)

flashcards.question: Tidal Range
flashcards.answer: The vertical difference between a high tide and the adjacent low tide

flashcards.question: Semidiurnal Tide
flashcards.answer: A tidal pattern with two high tides and two low tides per day

quiz_questions.question_text: Tides are primarily caused by:

options.text: The gravitational pull of the Moon and Sun

quiz_questions.explanation: The Moon's gravity (and to a lesser extent the Sun's) pulls on Earth's ocean water, creating tidal bulges.

quiz_questions.question_text: Spring tides occur during:

options.text: New moon and full moon (Sun and Moon aligned)

quiz_questions.explanation: 'Spring' refers to the tides 'springing forth'—when Sun and Moon are aligned, their combined gravity produces the largest tidal ranges.

quiz_questions.question_text: Neap tides have the _____ tidal range.
quiz_questions.explanation: During neap tides, the Sun and Moon pull at right angles, partially canceling each other and producing the smallest tides.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Moon has a greater tidal effect than the Sun because:

options.text: The Sun is too far away to matter
The Moon is much closer to Earth, and tidal force depends heavily on distance

quiz_questions.explanation: Tidal force is inversely proportional to the cube of distance. Despite being less massive, the Moon's proximity gives it about twice the Sun's tidal force.

quiz_questions.question_text: On most coasts, there are approximately:

options.text: Two high tides and two low tides per day

quiz_questions.explanation: Most coasts experience semidiurnal tides—two high and two low tides roughly every 24 hours and 50 minutes.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Bay of Fundy is famous for:

options.text: The world's largest tidal range (~16 m)

quiz_questions.explanation: The Bay of Fundy in Canada has tides up to 16 m due to its funnel-shaped geography that amplifies tidal forces.

quiz_questions.question_text: Spring tides and neap tides each occur approximately:
quiz_questions.explanation: Spring tides occur at new and full moons, and neap tides at first and third quarters—so each type happens about twice per lunar month.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Marine Ecosystems
summary_sections.content_html:
Earth's oceans contain diverse ecosystems, each with unique conditions and organisms:

  • Coral reefs: warm, shallow tropical waters; built by coral polyps with symbiotic algae (zooxanthellae); high biodiversity ('rainforests of the sea')
  • Estuaries: where rivers meet the ocean; brackish water; highly productive nursery habitats
  • Kelp forests: temperate coastal waters; giant kelp provides habitat for hundreds of species
  • Open ocean (pelagic zone): vast but relatively low productivity; phytoplankton are the primary producers
  • Deep-sea ecosystems: dark, cold, high pressure; organisms rely on marine snow or chemosynthesis at hydrothermal vents
  • Intertidal zone: coastline exposed between tides; organisms must tolerate air exposure, wave action, and salinity changes

flashcards.question: Coral Reef
flashcards.answer: A marine ecosystem built by coral polyps in warm, shallow tropical water; extremely high biodiversity

flashcards.question: Estuary
flashcards.answer: A partially enclosed coastal area where fresh river water mixes with saltwater; highly productive

flashcards.question: Kelp Forest
flashcards.answer: A temperate marine ecosystem dominated by large brown algae (kelp) that provides habitat for many species

flashcards.question: Phytoplankton
flashcards.answer: Microscopic photosynthetic organisms that are the base of most marine food webs

flashcards.question: Chemosynthesis
flashcards.answer: The production of energy by organisms using chemical reactions rather than sunlight, found at hydrothermal vents

quiz_questions.question_text: Coral reefs are sometimes called 'rainforests of the sea' because:

options.text: They are found in rainy climates

quiz_questions.explanation: Coral reefs cover less than 1% of the ocean floor but support roughly 25% of all marine species—rivaling tropical rainforest biodiversity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Estuaries are highly productive ecosystems because:

options.text: Nutrient-rich river water mixes with ocean water, supporting abundant life

quiz_questions.explanation: The mixing of nutrient-laden freshwater with seawater creates ideal conditions for plankton growth, making estuaries some of the most productive ecosystems.

quiz_questions.question_text: Deep-sea organisms near hydrothermal vents rely on _____ instead of photosynthesis.
quiz_questions.explanation: Chemosynthetic bacteria at vents convert hydrogen sulfide and other chemicals into energy, forming the base of these sunless ecosystems.

quiz_questions.question_text: The primary producers in the open ocean are:
quiz_questions.explanation: Phytoplankton are microscopic photosynthesizers that produce roughly half of Earth's oxygen and form the base of ocean food webs.

quiz_questions.question_text: Intertidal organisms must adapt to:

options.text: Changing conditions: exposure to air, waves, temperature shifts, and salinity changes

quiz_questions.explanation: The intertidal zone alternates between being submerged and exposed, requiring organisms to tolerate dramatic environmental changes.

quiz_questions.question_text: Kelp forests are primarily found in:
quiz_questions.explanation: Giant kelp thrives in cold, nutrient-rich waters along temperate coasts, forming underwater forests that shelter diverse marine life.

quiz_questions.question_text: Zooxanthellae are important to coral reefs because:

options.text: They are symbiotic algae that provide corals with nutrients through photosynthesis

quiz_questions.explanation: Zooxanthellae live inside coral tissues, photosynthesizing and providing up to 90% of the coral's energy needs.
